title: GUIDELINES ON RECOGNITION AND MAINTENANCE OF EQUIVALENCE OF NATIONAL FOOD CONTROL SYSTEMS (NFCS) CXG 101-2023

sections.section: GUIDELINES ON RECOGNITION AND MAINTENANCE OF EQUIVALENCE OF NATIONAL FOOD CONTROL SYSTEMS (NFCS) CXG 101-2023
sections.summary: Document title and adoption: 'Guidelines on Recognition and Maintenance of Equivalence of National Food Control Systems (NFCS) CXG 101-2023', adopted in 2023.

Recognizing equivalence of all or part of an exporting country's national food control system (NFCS) can reduce duplicate controls while protecting consumer health and fair trade, may change trade conditions (e.g., eligible establishment lists, alternative procedures, reduced port inspections), should be read with other Codex texts, and is independent of any reciprocal recognition process.

These guidelines give practical guidance for importing and exporting countries on assessing, recognising and maintaining equivalence of the whole or relevant parts of NFCSs at the system level, where requests may address consumer health protection, fair trade practices, or both.

Defines key terms used in the guidelines: 'decision criteria' (factors to determine if an exporting NFCS achieves the importing country's objectives), 'equivalence of NFCS' (capability of different NFCSs or parts to achieve the same objectives), and 'outcome' (intended effects contributing to those objectives).

Sets principles for considering NFCS equivalence including recognition that differently designed systems can achieve the same objectives, use of experience/assessments and alignment with international standards, assessment requirements (transparent, evidence-based, outcome-focused) and the need to document any recognition and its maintenance.

Assessment should evaluate whether the importing country's NFCS objectives and related outcomes or level of protection are achieved and be documented, transparent, evidence-based, outcome-focused, efficient, cooperative and timely.
Importing and exporting countries must document any recognition of equivalence, including how it will be implemented and maintained for trade between them.

Outlines a seven-step process for considering, assessing, recognising and maintaining NFCS equivalence: initial discussions; description of importing country's NFCS/objectives; decision criteria; description of exporting country's NFCS; assessment; decision; and final documentation.

sections.section: 5.1 STEP 1: INITIAL DISCUSSIONS, SCOPE AND DECISION TO COMMENCE
sections.summary: Section heading only—introduces Step 1 (initial discussions, scope and decision to commence) but contains no substantive provisions in this entry.

Prior to formal consultations, competent authorities are recommended to hold initial discussions to decide whether to pursue an equivalence assessment or an alternative mechanism and to cover matters such as legal frameworks, likely trade benefits, scope of the request, experience and trade history, development and design of NFCSs, relevant international standards, and any prior information exchanges or assessments.

sections.section: 5.2 STEP 2: DESCRIPTION OF THE IMPORTING COUNTRY'S NFCS AND THE RELEVANT OBJECTIVES
sections.summary: The importing country should provide, as relevant to the request scope, descriptive information and references on its NFCS objectives and elements (e.g., regulatory framework, control/approval requirements, verification/audit, monitoring/surveillance, enforcement, stakeholder communication, and monitoring/evaluation) to facilitate the exporting country's response.

sections.section: 5.3 STEP 3: THE DECISION CRITERIA FOR COMPARISON
sections.summary: After a formal request, the importing country must document decision criteria tied to its objectives and outcomes and share them with the exporting country; criteria (qualitative or quantitative) should indicate expected evidence levels, outcome indicators, level of protection and use of experience/confidence, focus on system performance, and must not demand a higher standard than the importing country's NFCS.

sections.section: 5.4 STEP 4: DESCRIPTION OF EXPORTING COUNTRY'S NFCS OR RELEVANT PART
sections.summary: The exporting country should supply evidence and references describing its NFCS or relevant part to demonstrate how it achieves the importing country's objectives and level of protection, with importing countries allowing practical flexibility in submission format and requesting additional detail where necessary.

The importing country's assessment must use a transparent, evidence-based methodology focused on whether the exporting country's NFCS meets the decision criteria, including checking information sufficiency, requesting additional evidence, proposing or considering additional controls, using outcome indicators, weighting elements by impact, and conducting document reviews and in‑country assessments where justified; assessments should be cooperative, timely, protect confidentiality, avoid conflicts of interest, be transparent, consider system resilience and resources, and may be supported by meetings or technical assistance.

The decision process must be transparent and timely, focus on the agreed decision criteria without introducing extra objectives, document draft conclusions for exporting-country comment, allow additional information or corrective plans if initially assessed not equivalent, and record final conclusions and rationale.

Any recognition should be documented (e.g., letters or agreements) to specify implementation measures and include provisions for maintenance and review—defining what changes trigger notification or review—and set expectations for ongoing communication, performance reporting, technical discussions and intermittent country visits or exchanges.
Non-substantive bibliographic notes listing referenced Codex guidelines and guidance documents.
